children.name: Clupea
children.description: Atlantic and Pacific herrings — the most commercially important fish in the world by total catch. Baltic herring (strömming) and Atlantic herring (sill) are central to Swedish food culture in a way that transcends cuisine: fermented Baltic herring (surströmming) is a legally protected Swedish cultural delicacy whose annual opening season triggers international headlines. Herring move in vast, coordinated shoals of millions of fish, a defensive anti-predator strategy that creates one of the most spectacular wildlife phenomena in the sea — the 'bait balls' that attract gannets, whales, and tuna simultaneously. Their fat content varies dramatically through the year, peaking in autumn when they are harvested for pickling and fermentation.

children.name: Clupea harengus
children.description: The Baltic subspecies (C. h. membras) is smaller and less fatty than the North Sea herring — this is the strömming of Swedish cuisine, sold fermented as surströmming or pickled as inlagd sill. One of the most important prey fish for cod, seals, and seabirds in the North and Baltic Seas.

children.name: Clupea pallasii
children.description: The Pacific counterpart of the Atlantic herring and equally commercially important. Herring roe (kazunoko) is a prized New Year food in Japan. Spawning events in British Columbia turn whole bays bright green with eggs and attract eagles, sea lions, and grey whales.

children.name: Clupea bentincki
children.description: A small herring endemic to the cool, upwelling-rich waters off Chile and Peru. A major forage fish of the Humboldt Current and an important component of Chile's pelagic fishery.

children.name: Sprattus
children.description: Sprats — small, herring-like clupeids of northern European and southern hemisphere seas. The Baltic sprat (skarpsill) forms one of the largest fish populations in the Baltic Sea and is the primary food fish for Baltic cod, salmon, and sea trout. Sprats are also heavily fished for human consumption, smoked and canned in Scandinavia (ansjovis — despite the name, these are sprats, not anchovies). The sprat's relationship with the Baltic cod is a classic predator-prey cycle: when cod collapsed in the 1980s–90s due to overfishing, sprat populations exploded, crashing the zooplankton communities that their larvae depended on.

children.name: Sprattus sprattus
children.description: The Baltic subspecies (S. s. balticus) is the most abundant fish in the Baltic Sea by biomass. Critical prey for cod, salmon, and seabirds. Canned smoked sprats are sold as 'ansjovis' across Scandinavia and are a classic Swedish pizza topping.

children.name: Alosa
children.description: Shads — large, anadromous clupeids that migrate from the sea into rivers to spawn, similar in life history to salmon. Allis shad and twaite shad once ran up European rivers in extraordinary numbers; like salmon, they were blocked by weirs and dams and decimated by river pollution through the 19th and 20th centuries. The twaite shad (staksill in Swedish) migrates into rivers including the Rhine and is the subject of active restoration programs in Britain and Germany. American shad (Alosa sapidissima) was once the most important food fish on the east coast of North America — 'sapidissima' means 'most delicious'.

children.name: Alosa fallax
children.description: Vulnerable in Europe; makes spring spawning migrations into major rivers. The Wye and Severn rivers in Britain hold the most important UK populations. Can produce audible sounds by vibrating its swimbladder during courtship. Very sensitive to river barriers.

children.name: Alosa alosa
children.description: The largest European shad, anadromous, migrating from the Atlantic into rivers to spawn. Once abundant in the Rhine, Loire and Garonne but now Vulnerable due to dams and pollution; the Gironde-Garonne-Dordogne system holds a key remaining population.

children.name: Alosa sapidissima
children.description: Anadromous shad of the North American Atlantic coast; the specific epithet means 'most delicious'. Once the most economically important food fish on the US east coast, it was introduced to the Pacific coast in the 1870s where it became established.

children.name: Alosa pseudoharengus
children.description: Small anadromous shad of eastern North America that has formed landlocked populations in the Great Lakes, where it became invasive and disrupted native food webs. A key forage fish historically used as fertilizer and bait.

children.name: Alosa aestivalis
children.description: Anadromous river herring of the US Atlantic coast, similar to the alewife but spawning later in warmer water. Populations have declined sharply from overfishing and dams, prompting fishing moratoriums in several states.

children.name: Alosa immaculata
children.description: Anadromous shad of the Black Sea and Sea of Azov that migrates up the Danube to spawn. An important commercial fish in the lower Danube; its long-distance river migrations are threatened by dams.

children.name: Alosa tanaica
children.description: A small anadromous shad of the Black Sea and Sea of Azov basins that ascends rivers such as the Don and Dnieper to spawn. Locally important to fisheries and sensitive to river damming.

children.name: Sardina
children.description: The European pilchard (sardine) — a single-species genus of the Mediterranean and eastern Atlantic. Mature fish are sold as pilchards, young fish as sardines. One of the most heavily fished species in Europe and a cornerstone of the Portuguese, Spanish and Mediterranean canning industries.

children.name: Sardina pilchardus
children.description: The classic canned sardine of the eastern Atlantic and Mediterranean. Grilled sardines are an icon of Portuguese summer festivals. A vital forage fish whose populations fluctuate with ocean temperature and upwelling.

children.name: Sardinops
children.description: The pilchards or 'true sardines' of the Pacific and Indian Oceans, often treated as a single circumglobal species with regional populations. The South African sardine run is one of the greatest marine migrations on Earth, with billions of fish followed by dolphins, sharks, gannets and whales.

children.name: Sardinops sagax
children.description: A circumglobal pilchard of cool, productive waters off South America, southern Africa, Australia, Japan and California. Its enormous, climate-driven boom-and-bust cycles drive entire fisheries; the South African 'sardine run' is a world-famous wildlife spectacle.

children.name: Sardinella
children.description: Sardinellas — a large genus of small, fast-growing tropical and subtropical clupeids. They dominate artisanal and commercial fisheries across West Africa, the Indian Ocean and Southeast Asia, providing critical protein and income for coastal communities.

children.name: Sardinella aurita
children.description: A widespread warm-water sardinella of both sides of the Atlantic and the Mediterranean. The most important small pelagic fishery off West Africa, where it is critical to food security; subject to growing concern over overfishing and warming seas.

children.name: Sardinella longiceps
children.description: One of the most important fishes of the Indian Ocean, supporting a massive fishery off India's southwest coast. Its abundance swings dramatically with the monsoon-driven upwelling, making catches highly variable year to year.

children.name: Sardinella gibbosa
children.description: A small Indo-West Pacific sardinella marked by a faint golden lateral stripe. One of the most commonly landed sardines across South and Southeast Asia, eaten fresh, dried and canned.

children.name: Brevoortia
children.description: Menhaden — deep-bodied, oily clupeids of the Atlantic and Gulf coasts of the Americas. Rarely eaten directly, they are caught in huge volumes for fish meal, oil and omega-3 supplements, and are ecologically vital as filter feeders and as prey for striped bass and ospreys.

children.name: Brevoortia tyrannus
children.description: An extremely abundant filter-feeding clupeid of the US Atlantic coast, central to the largest fishery by volume on the east coast. Called 'the most important fish in the sea' for its role linking plankton to predators like striped bass, tuna and ospreys.

children.name: Brevoortia patronus
children.description: The dominant menhaden of the Gulf of Mexico and basis of one of the largest fisheries in the USA by weight. Harvested almost entirely for fish meal and fish oil reduction.

children.name: Tenualosa
children.description: Tropical shads of South and Southeast Asia, including the hilsa — an anadromous fish of immense cultural and economic value in Bangladesh and eastern India, where it is the national fish of Bangladesh.

children.name: Tenualosa ilisha
children.description: The national fish of Bangladesh and the most economically important single fish species of the country. Anadromous, migrating from the Bay of Bengal up the Ganges-Brahmaputra and other rivers; prized for its rich, oily flesh and central to Bengali cuisine and identity.

children.name: Dorosoma
children.description: Gizzard shads of North and Central America — freshwater and estuarine clupeids named for their muscular, gizzard-like stomach used to grind detritus and plankton. They are crucial forage fish in reservoirs and rivers, often dominating fish biomass.

children.name: Dorosoma cepedianum
children.description: A widespread filter-feeding shad of North American rivers, lakes and reservoirs. A vital forage fish for bass, walleye and catfish; its winterkills and population booms strongly shape freshwater food webs.

children.name: Opisthonema
children.description: Thread herrings of the Americas, named for the long, trailing last ray of the dorsal fin. They are important coastal pelagic fish in the eastern Pacific and western Atlantic, fished for canning, bait and reduction to fish meal.

children.name: Opisthonema oglinum
children.description: A schooling coastal clupeid of the western Atlantic from the USA to Brazil, recognizable by its elongated last dorsal-fin ray. Widely used as bait and increasingly harvested for fish meal.

children.name: Ethmalosa
children.description: The bonga shad — a single-species genus of estuarine West African clupeid that tolerates a wide range of salinity. It is one of the most important fishes for West African artisanal fisheries, eaten smoked and dried.

children.name: Ethmalosa fimbriata
children.description: An estuarine filter-feeding clupeid ranging along West Africa from Mauritania to Angola. A staple of West African artisanal fisheries, traditionally smoked; populations are under pressure from intensive fishing and habitat change.

children.name: Harengula
children.description: Scaled sardines or pilchards of the warm western Atlantic. Small, silvery, schooling fish of coastal and estuarine waters, they are among the most important baitfish for sport and commercial fishing in the Caribbean and Gulf of Mexico.

children.name: Harengula jaguana
children.description: An abundant inshore clupeid of the western Atlantic from the USA to Brazil, common over seagrass and sandy bottoms. A premier live bait for tarpon, snook and other gamefish.

children.name: Hilsa
children.description: Kelee shads of the Indo-Pacific — coastal and estuarine clupeids related to the hilsa. Distinguished by dark spots behind the gill cover; they support local fisheries across the Indian Ocean and western Pacific.

children.name: Hilsa kelee
children.description: A coastal shad of the Indo-West Pacific, identified by a row of dark blotches along the upper flank. Caught throughout its range in artisanal fisheries and valued as food.

children.name: Etrumeus
children.description: Round herrings — slender, cylindrical clupeids of warm and temperate seas worldwide, distinguished from typical herrings by their rounded belly lacking sharp scutes. They form large offshore schools and support purse-seine fisheries.

children.name: Etrumeus sadina
children.description: A slender, round-bodied herring of the western Atlantic, named for its conspicuous red eye. Forms dense offshore schools and is harvested for bait and food.

children.name: Etrumeus teres
children.description: A widespread Indo-Pacific round herring forming large offshore schools. Targeted by purse-seine fisheries and an important forage fish for tuna and other predators.

children.name: Nematalosa
children.description: Gizzard shads of the Indo-Pacific, with a muscular gizzard-like stomach and a trailing last dorsal ray. They inhabit coastal, estuarine and freshwater habitats and are caught locally for food.

children.name: Nematalosa nasus
children.description: A bottom-feeding gizzard shad widespread across the Indo-West Pacific, tolerant of brackish and fresh water. Common in estuaries and supports artisanal fisheries throughout southern Asia.

children.name: Escualosa
children.description: Whitefin or river sardines — small, delicate silvery clupeids of Indo-Pacific coasts and rivers, often forming dense inshore schools that are dip-netted for food and bait.

children.name: Escualosa thoracata
children.description: A tiny silvery clupeid of Indo-Pacific coastal and estuarine waters, often appearing in huge inshore shoals. Caught in large numbers for drying and as a delicacy when fresh.

children.name: Kowala
children.description: The slender white sardine — a single-species Indo-Pacific genus of small coastal clupeid, distinguished by serrated belly scutes and a slim body.

children.name: Kowala coval
children.description: A small schooling clupeid of Indo-West Pacific coastal waters with strongly keeled belly scutes. Caught in coastal seines and consumed fresh or dried across South and Southeast Asia.

children.name: Amblygaster
children.description: Smoothbelly sardinellas of the Indo-Pacific, similar to Sardinella but lacking sharp belly scutes. They form large offshore schools and are important to tropical small-pelagic fisheries.

children.name: Amblygaster sirm
children.description: A warm-water sardinella of the Indo-West Pacific marked by a row of golden spots along the flank. Forms large schools and is a major target of tropical purse-seine and ring-net fisheries.

children.name: Gudusia
children.description: Freshwater river shads of South Asia, inhabiting the Ganges and other large river systems and floodplain lakes. They are important food fish in inland fisheries of the Indian subcontinent.

children.name: Gudusia chapra
children.description: A small freshwater clupeid of the rivers, canals and floodplain lakes of South Asia, abundant in the Ganges-Brahmaputra basin. A common, inexpensive food fish in inland fisheries of India and Bangladesh.
